sections.heading: Networking is Not About collecting Contacts
sections.body:
Networking = building genuine, mutually beneficial relationships.

It's NOT:
• Collecting business cards
• Asking for favors immediately
• Talking only about yourself

It IS:
• Being genuinely curious about others
• Finding ways to help before asking for help
• Building trust over time

Key principle: Give first, ask later. The best networkers are givers, not takers.

sections.heading: Where to Network
sections.body:
Online:
• LinkedIn (professional updates, thoughtful comments)
• Twitter/X (industry discussions)
• Online communities (Slack groups, Discord servers)

Offline:
• Industry events and conferences
• Meetups and workshops
• Alumni events
• Volunteering
• Co-working spaces

Daily:
• Your current colleagues
• Neighbors and community members
• Parents at your kids' school
• People at the gym or coffee shop

sections.heading: The Follow-Up Formula
sections.body:
Meeting someone new:
1. Connect within 24 hours (LinkedIn or email)
2. Reference something specific from your conversation
3. Offer something of value (article, introduction, resource)
4. Suggest a specific next step

Example:
'Hi [Name], it was great meeting you at [event]. I really enjoyed our conversation about [topic]. I found this article that might be useful: [link]. Would you be open to grabbing coffee sometime?'

Keep in touch:
• Share relevant articles occasionally
• Congratulate on achievements
• Check in every 2-3 months
• Remember personal details (birthdays, hobbies)

jargon.definition: Building and maintaining professional relationships for mutual benefit. Most opportunities come through personal connections, not job boards.
A 30-60 second summary of who you are, what you do, and what you're looking for. Named because it should fit in an elevator ride.
Acquaintances you don't know well. Research shows weak ties often provide the most job opportunities — they connect you to different networks.

summary: Networking is about building genuine relationships, not collecting contacts. Give value first, follow up consistently, and nurture connections over time. Most opportunities come through people.
